The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

10	# The `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` substrate (A.1.c.SubT)
11	
12	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
14	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
15	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
16	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).
17	
18	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
19	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
20	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
21	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
22	
23	1. a binary tensor-product operation
24	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
25	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
26	3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
27	   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.
28	
29	At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
30	is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
31	as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
32	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
33	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
34	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
35	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.
36	
37	## Status (iter-202 Lane TS — file-skeleton scaffold)

39	This file is the **iter-202 Lane TS** file-skeleton: each of the 4 pinned

47	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

95	
96	The presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor along a morphism of
97	presheaves of *commutative* rings is lax monoidal. Mathlib ships the sectionwise
98	fact `ModuleCat.restrictScalars f` is `LaxMonoidal`
99	(`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction`); here we lift it to
100	the presheaf level through the sectionwise presheaf monoidal structure
101	(`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal`). This is the sole project-side ingredient feeding
102	the oplax comparison `δ` of `pullback φ` (the mate of `pushforward φ`) used to
103	close `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. Per blueprint `lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal`. -/

111	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
112	/-- The lax-monoidal unit `ε` of `restrictScalars α`, assembled sectionwise from
113	`ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X)`'s lax-monoidal unit. -/
114	noncomputable def restrictScalarsLaxε
115	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :
116	    (𝟙_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))) ⟶
117	      (restrictScalars α).obj (𝟙_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (S ⋙ forget₂ _ _))) where
118	  app X := Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X).hom)
119	  naturality {X Y} f := by
120	    ext r
121	    dsimp
122	    erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one, ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η,
123	      ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η]
124	    simp only [map_one]
125	    erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one]
126

128	/-- The lax-monoidal tensorator `μ` of `restrictScalars α`, assembled sectionwise
129	from `ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X)`'s lax-monoidal tensorator. -/
130	noncomputable def restrictScalarsLaxμ
131	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
132	    (M₁ M₂ : PresheafOfModules.{u} (S ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) :
133	    (restrictScalars α).obj M₁ ⊗ (restrictScalars α).obj M₂ ⟶
134	      (restrictScalars α).obj (M₁ ⊗ M₂) where
135	  app X := by
136	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X).hom) (M₁.obj X) (M₂.obj X)
137	  naturality {X Y} f := by
138	    refine ModuleCat.MonoidalCategory.tensor_ext (fun m₁ m₂ ↦ ?_)
139	    dsimp
140	    erw [PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul, ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul,
141	      ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
142	    rfl

146	commutative rings. Project-local lift of `ModuleCat.instLaxMonoidalRestrictScalars`. -/
147	noncomputable instance restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal
148	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :
149	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars α).LaxMonoidal where
150	  ε := restrictScalarsLaxε α
151	  μ M₁ M₂ := restrictScalarsLaxμ α M₁ M₂
152	  μ_natural_left := by
153	    intro X Y f X'
154	    ext1 Z
155	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ_natural_left (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
156	      (f.app Z) (X'.obj Z)
157	  μ_natural_right := by
158	    intro X Y X' f
159	    ext1 Z
160	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ_natural_right (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
161	      (X'.obj Z) (f.app Z)
162	  associativity := by
163	    intro M N P
164	    ext1 Z
165	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.associativity (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
166	      (M.obj Z) (N.obj Z) (P.obj Z)

168	    intro M
169	    ext1 Z
170	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.left_unitality (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
171	      (M.obj Z)
172	  right_unitality := by
173	    intro M
174	    ext1 Z
175	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.right_unitality (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)

186	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
187	
188	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
189	
190	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
191	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
192	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
193	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
194	
195	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
196	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
197	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
198	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
199	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
200	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
201	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
202	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
203	
204	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
205	
206	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
207	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
208	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
209	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
210	
211	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
212	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
213	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
214	sheafification. -/
215	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
216	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
217	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
218	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
219	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
220	
221	/-! ## §2. (Off the critical path) the full monoidal-category structure
222	
223	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
224	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
225	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
226	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal
227	coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and no `MonoidalClosed` structure is ever
228	consumed. The earlier `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance (and the two
229	localization-transport supplements `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
230	`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) routed through the verified-absent
231	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall; they are removed here. The group
232	law is built directly on the line-bundle subcategory from the four
233	existence-of-iso lemmas below, mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`. -/
234	
235	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
236	
237	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
238	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
239	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
240	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
241	
242	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
243	
244	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
245	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
246	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
247	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
248	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
249	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
250	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
251	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
252	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
253	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
254	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
255	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
256	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
257	
258	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
259	sheaf.**
260	
261	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
262	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
263	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
264	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
265	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
266	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
267	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
268	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
269	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
270	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
271	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
272	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
273	
274	/-- **Refining a trivialisation to a smaller open.** If `M` is trivialised on an
275	open `U` (`M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U`), it is trivialised on every open `W ≤ U`.
276	
277	The chart-chase is identical in spirit to `LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`:
278	factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`
279	and `restrictFunctorComp` to identify `M.restrict W.ι` with
280	`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
281	`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
282	via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
283	because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
284	noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
285	    (hWU : W ≤ U)
286	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
287	    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
288	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
289	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
290	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]
291	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
292	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
293	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
294	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
295	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
296	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
297	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
298	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
299	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
300	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso
301	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M) ≪≫ ?_

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). The global
96	compatibility isomorphism is nonetheless available for \emph{arbitrary}
97	modules \(M, N\), because the relevant restriction is along an \emph{open
98	immersion}: restriction is then computed sectionwise, and the comparison of
99	structure-sheaf rings over the image is the \emph{isomorphism}
100	\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so the base change is extension of scalars along a ring
101	isomorphism --- which is automatically an isomorphism and commutes with
102	\(\otimes\). No line-bundle or flatness hypothesis is needed. This is
103	exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
104	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
105	\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
106	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
107	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
108	
109	\section{Mathlib API survey}
110	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
111	
112	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
113	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
114	
115	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
116	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
117	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
118	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
119	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
120	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
121	sectionwise tensor product
122	\[
123	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
124	\]
125	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
126	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
127	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
128	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
129	
130	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
132	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
133	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
134	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
135	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
136	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
138	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
139	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
140	tensor-product operation
141	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
142	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
143	
144	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
145	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
146	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
147	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
148	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
149	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
150	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
151	\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
152	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
153	for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
154	\(f\), the canonical comparison
155	\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
156	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
157	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
158	restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
159	isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
160	ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
161	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
162	project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
163	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
164	flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
165	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
166	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
167	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
168	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
169	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
170	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
171	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
172	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
173	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
174	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
175	independently of any upstream timing.
176	
177	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
178	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
179	
180	\begin{definition}
181	
182	\leanok
183	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
184	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
186	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
187	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
188	  \[
189	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
190	  \]
191	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
192	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
193	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
194	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
195	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
196	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
197	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
198	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
199	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
200	  canonical compatibility
201	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
202	     \;\cong\;
203	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
204	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
205	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
206	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
207	
208	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
209	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
210	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
211	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
212	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
213	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
214	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
215	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
216	\end{definition}

222	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}

224	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

226	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of

228	  \[
229	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
230	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
231	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
232	  \]
233	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
234	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
235	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
236	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
237	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data

239	  \[

241	    \qquad
242	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
243	  \]

245	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
246	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
247	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
248	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
249	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
250	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a

262	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under

268	\begin{remark}
269	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
270	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
271	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
272	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
273	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
274	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
275	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
276	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
277	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
278	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
279	  depend on it.
280	
281	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
282	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
283	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
284	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
285	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
286	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
287	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
288	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
289	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
290	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
291	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
292	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
293	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
294	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
295	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
296	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
297	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
298	
299	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
300	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
301	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
302	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
303	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
304	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
305	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
306	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
307	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
308	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
309	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
310	\end{remark}

312	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
313	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
314	
315	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
316	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
317	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
318	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
319	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
320	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
321	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
322	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
323	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
324	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
325	cover, together with their restriction to the
326	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
327	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
328	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
329	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

331	\begin{lemma}
332	
333	\leanok
334	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
335	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
336	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
337	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
338	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
339	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
340	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
341	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
342	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
343	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
344	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
345	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
346	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
347	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
348	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
349	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
350	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
351	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
352	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
353	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
354	  \[
355	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
356	      \;\;:=\;\;
357	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
358	  \]
359	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
360	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
361	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
362	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
363	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
364	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
365	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
370	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
371	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
372	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
373	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
374	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
375	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
376	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
377	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
378	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
379	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
380	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
381	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
382	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
383	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
384	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
385	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
386	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
387	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
388	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
389	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
390	
391	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
392	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
393	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
394	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
395	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
396	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
397	\end{proof}

399	\begin{lemma}
400	
401	\leanok
402	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
403	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
405	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
406	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
407	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
408	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
409	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
410	  morphism
411	  \[
412	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
413	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
414	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
415	  \]
416	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
417	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
418	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
419	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
420	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is

422	  target is
423	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).

429	  % "sectionwise unfolding of PresheafOfModules.pullback varphi (~30--60 lines)"
430	  % does NOT exist: that functor is the opaque abstract left adjoint of

432	  % the opacity, then assumes an unfolding anyway). The genuine residual needs
433	  % TWO project-side, Mathlib-ABSENT ingredients (~200--300 LOC, mathlib-build
434	  % scale): (H1) a PRESHEAF-level iso `pushforward beta ~= pullback varphi` via
435	  % Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq from a presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
436	  % (needs presheaf pushforwardNatTrans + pushforwardCongr; only SHEAF versions
437	  % exist), and (H2) strong-monoidal `restrictScalars` along the ring iso
438	  % f.appIso (upgrade the file's lax `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` to strong;
439	  % bottoms out in the absent "restrictScalars along a ring iso is strong
440	  % monoidal"). Steps 1--2 (restrictFunctorIsoPullback, sheafificationCompPullback,
441	  % strip outer sheafification via mapIso) ARE formalized and GREEN. Step 3 and
442	  % the closing "30--60 line helper" claim must be rewritten to the H1/H2
443	  % decomposition before this is re-attempted. See
444	  % informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md and task_results for the corrected route.
445	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
446	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
447	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
448	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
449	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
450	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
451	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
452	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
453	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
454	  isomorphism}
455	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
456	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
457	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
458	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
459	  therefore extension of scalars along
460	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
461	
462	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
463	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
464	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
465	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
466	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
467	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
468	  the abstract pullback functor.
469	
470	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
471	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
472	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
473	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
474	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
475	   \;\cong\;
476	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
477	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
478	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
479	  comparison
480	  \[
481	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
482	      \;\longrightarrow\;
483	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
484	      \;\otimes\;
485	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
486	  \]
487	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
488	
489	  \emph{Step 3 (sectionwise identification along the open-immersion ring
490	  isomorphism).} The functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is
491	  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of pushforward and is consequently
492	  \emph{opaque} on objects and morphisms: Mathlib provides no sectionwise
493	  formula for it, and --- unlike the scheme-level restriction --- no
494	  presheaf-level analogue of \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). The one
495	  genuinely-new project-side ingredient of this proof is therefore a
496	  \emph{sectionwise unfolding} of the abstract pullback along the open
497	  immersion: for the open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\), the presheaf
498	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,P\) is, section by section over an
499	  open \(V\), the base change of the sections of \(P\) along the ring
500	  \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\) --- that is, restriction of
501	  sections combined with scalar transport across the isomorphism. (Over an open
502	  immersion the sections themselves agree definitionally,
503	  \(\Gamma(M|_f, V) = \Gamma(M, f(V))\) by the \(\mathtt{restrict\_obj}\)
504	  identity; only the scalar ring is transported, and it is transported along an
505	  isomorphism.)
506	
507	  Granting this sectionwise description, the base-change comparison of Step 2
508	  is, on each section over \(V\), the canonical map of a tensor product under
509	  base change along the ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Base change
510	  along a ring isomorphism is a \emph{monoidal equivalence} of module
511	  categories --- it is invertible and carries tensor products to tensor products
512	  --- by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\) applied to
513	  the ring equivalence underlying \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Hence each
514	  sectionwise comparison is an isomorphism compatible with \(\otimes\). No
515	  flatness, no \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)-monoidality, and no strong-monoidal
516	  pullback instance is invoked: the entire force of the step is that the scalar
517	  change is along an \emph{isomorphism}.
518	
519	  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise isomorphisms of Step 3 are natural in \(V\)
520	  (compatible with the presheaf restriction maps, the naturality square being
521	  that of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) against restriction). They therefore assemble,
522	  via \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\), into a single presheaf-of-modules

525	  restriction--pullback comparison of Step 1 yields the asserted global
526	  isomorphism \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\).

528	  The route uses no monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) or on the
529	  pullback functor, no flatness, and no line-bundle hypothesis. The single
530	  bounded project-side ingredient is the sectionwise unfolding of
531	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along an open immersion (a

536	  (\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\),
537	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\),
538	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\),
539	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\)) is already present in Mathlib.

594	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules) applies and both sides restrict to the
595	  iterated tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules on \(U_i\). There the associator
596	  \((L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i) \otimes_{A_i} N_i \cong
597	  L_i \otimes_{A_i} (M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i)\) is the standard associativity
598	  isomorphism of the tensor product of modules over a commutative ring. These
599	  local isomorphisms agree on overlaps --- naturality of the module
600	  associator against the restriction-comparison isomorphisms of
601	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and therefore glue to a global
602	  isomorphism. Only the \emph{existence} of the global isomorphism is
603	  asserted; no compatibility with a pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
604	\end{proof}

608	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}

611	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) a line
612	  bundle. Then \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\) and
613	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong L\); equivalently
614	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L)\) holds (and
615	  symmetrically on the right).

621	  commutes with \(\otimes_X\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and the
622	  structure sheaf restricts to \(A_i\) as an \(A_i\)-module; the unitor
623	  \(A_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i \cong L_i\) is the standard left-unit isomorphism of
624	  the tensor product. These local isomorphisms glue to a global isomorphism
625	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\); the right-unit case is identical.

637	\end{lemma}

642	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}); locally the braiding
643	  \(L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i \cong M_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i\) is the standard
644	  symmetry isomorphism of the tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules. The local
645	  symmetries glue to a global isomorphism.

660	  \]
661	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
662	  isomorphism
663	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
664	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
665	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
666	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
667	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
668	\end{lemma}

671	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
672	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
673	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
674	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
675	  The contraction map

679	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
680	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
681	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
682	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
683	\end{proof}

745	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
746	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
747	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
748	  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet; blocked on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso.
749	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
750	  functor
751	  \[
752	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
753	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
754	  \]
755	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
756	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
757	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
758	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
759	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
760	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
761	  canonical isomorphism
762	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
763	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
764	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
765	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
766	\end{lemma}
767	
768	\begin{proof}
769	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
770	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
771	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
772	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
773	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
774	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
775	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
776	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
777	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
778	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
779	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
780	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
781	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
782	  same cover.
783	\end{proof}

785	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
786	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
787	
788	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
789	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
790	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
791	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
792	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
793	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
794	
795	\begin{theorem}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
799	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
801	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
802	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
803	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
804	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
805	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
806	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
807	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
808	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
809	
810	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
811	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
812	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
813	  % groups)
814	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
815	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
816	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
817	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
818	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
819	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
820	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
821	  % \end{dfn}
822	  %  [\dots]
823	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
824	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
825	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
826	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
827	  % topologies  by
828	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
829	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
830	  %  \end{dfn}"
831	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
832	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
833	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
834	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
835	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
836	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
837	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
838	  \[
839	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
840	  \]
841	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
842	  abelian-group structure with addition
843	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
844	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
845	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
846	  inverse
847	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
848	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
849	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
850	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
851	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
852	  map
853	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
854	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
855	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
856	\end{theorem}
857	
858	\begin{proof}
859	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
860	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
861	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
862	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
863	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
864	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
865	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
866	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
867	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
868	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
869	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
870	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
871	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
872	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
873	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
874	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
875	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
876	  read on isomorphism classes:
877	  \begin{itemize}
878	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
879	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
880	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
881	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
882	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
883	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
884	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
885	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
886	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
887	  \end{itemize}
888	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
889	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
890	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
891	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
892	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
893	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
894	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
895	  transported from
896	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
897	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
898	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
899	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
900	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
901	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
902	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
903	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
904	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
905	  verbatim.
906	
907	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
908	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
909	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
910	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
911	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
912	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
913	
914	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
915	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
916	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
917	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
918	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
919	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
920	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
921	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
922	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
923	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
924	\end{proof}
